dosAndDonts.title: Always say 'Bonjour' when entering a shop
dosAndDonts.explanation: In France, greeting the shopkeeper is expected. Not saying 'Bonjour' is considered very rude. Even in supermarkets, greet the cashier.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't skip the 'Bonjour' before asking a question
dosAndDonts.explanation: Never start a conversation with a stranger without first saying 'Bonjour.' Even asking for directions requires a greeting first. Jumping straight to your question is considered impolite.

dosAndDonts.title: Use 'vous' with strangers and older people
dosAndDonts.explanation: French has formal (vous) and informal (tu) forms of 'you.' Always use 'vous' with professors, shopkeepers, strangers, and anyone older than you. Wait for them to invite you to use 'tu.'

dosAndDonts.title: Don't use 'tu' with your professor
dosAndDonts.explanation: Using 'tu' (informal you) with a professor is a serious social mistake. Always address professors with 'vous' and their title (Professeur/Madame/Monsieur).

dosAndDonts.title: La bise (cheek kisses) with friends
dosAndDonts.explanation: Close friends greet each other with cheek kisses (la bise). The number varies by region: 2 in Paris, 3 in Provence, sometimes 4! Watch what locals do. Don't initiate with strangers.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't eat on public transport
dosAndDonts.explanation: Eating on the metro, bus, or tram is frowned upon in France. It's considered messy and inconsiderate. Wait until you're at your destination or find a bench.

dosAndDonts.title: Keep your voice down in public places
dosAndDonts.explanation: French people tend to speak more softly in public. Speaking loudly on the phone or in the metro is considered rude. Be mindful of your volume, especially on trains.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't rush through meals
dosAndDonts.explanation: Meals are a social ritual in France. Lunch can last 1-2 hours. Don't eat too quickly or leave immediately after finishing. Enjoy the conversation and take your time.

dosAndDonts.title: Say 'Au revoir' when leaving a shop
dosAndDonts.explanation: Just as you greet when entering, always say goodbye when leaving. 'Au revoir' or 'Bonne journée' (have a good day) shows respect.

dosAndDonts.title: Bring a small gift when invited to someone's home
dosAndDonts.explanation: When invited for dinner, bring wine, chocolates, or flowers. Never bring chrysanthemums (funeral flowers) or an even number of flowers. A bottle of wine is always appreciated.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't arrive exactly on time for dinner parties
dosAndDonts.explanation: For social gatherings (not business), arriving 10-15 minutes late is perfectly acceptable and even expected. Arriving exactly on time might catch your host unprepared.

dosAndDonts.title: Try to speak French, even if imperfect
dosAndDonts.explanation: French people greatly appreciate when foreigners make an effort to speak French. Even starting with 'Bonjour, je ne parle pas très bien français' (I don't speak French very well) goes a long way.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't discuss money or salaries openly
dosAndDonts.explanation: Asking someone how much they earn is very personal in France. Avoid questions about income, rent, or the cost of someone's belongings. It's considered tactless.

dosAndDonts.title: Respect the lunch break
dosAndDonts.explanation: Many shops and offices close between 12:00-14:00 for lunch. Don't expect everything to be open during this time. Plan your errands outside lunch hours.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't expect shops to be open on Sundays
dosAndDonts.explanation: Most shops are closed on Sundays, and many are closed on Mondays too. Supermarkets may have limited Sunday morning hours. Plan your shopping accordingly.

dosAndDonts.title: Keep bread on the table, not on your plate
dosAndDonts.explanation: In French dining, bread goes directly on the tablecloth/table, not on your side plate. Tear bread with your hands rather than cutting it with a knife.

dosAndDonts.title: Don't ask for ketchup in restaurants
dosAndDonts.explanation: Asking for ketchup, especially with a well-prepared dish, can be seen as insulting the chef. French cuisine values the original flavors. The exception is with fries (frites).

dosAndDonts.title: Always queue in an orderly line
dosAndDonts.explanation: The French respect queues. Cutting in line is extremely rude. If you're unsure who's last, ask 'C'est vous le dernier?' (Are you the last one?).

greetingStyles.greeting: Bonjour Professeur / Bonjour Madame / Bonjour Monsieur
greetingStyles.notes: Always use formal address. Wait for them to extend their hand first.

greetingStyles.situation: Meeting a classmate for the first time
greetingStyles.notes: Casual but polite. Once you become friends, you may switch to la bise.
Number of kisses varies by region (2 in Paris, 3 in south). Start from the right cheek.
Always greet when entering and say 'Au revoir' when leaving.
Professional and formal. Use last names until invited otherwise.

greetingStyles.greeting: Allô? Bonjour, c'est [your name] à l'appareil.
greetingStyles.notes: Always identify yourself. 'Allô' is only used on the phone, never in person.

cultureQuiz.scenario: You enter a small bakery in Paris. What do you do first?
options: Walk straight to the counter and point at what you want
Say 'Bonjour!' to the shopkeeper before ordering
Start browsing silently without making eye contact
Pull out your phone to check prices online
cultureQuiz.explanation: Always greet with 'Bonjour!' when entering any shop in France. This is one of the most important social rules. Not greeting is considered very rude.

cultureQuiz.scenario: Your professor asks you to email them about your assignment. How do you begin the email?
options: Bonjour Professeur, je me permets de vous contacter au sujet de...
cultureQuiz.explanation: French academic emails require formal language. Start with a proper greeting and the phrase 'je me permets de vous contacter' (I am writing to you). English-style casual greetings are inappropriate.

cultureQuiz.scenario: You're invited to a French friend's home for dinner at 8 PM. What time do you arrive?
cultureQuiz.explanation: For social dinners, arriving 10-15 minutes late is perfectly acceptable and even expected ('le quart d'heure de politesse'). Arriving early might catch your host unprepared.

cultureQuiz.scenario: A French classmate greets you with cheek kisses (la bise). What's the proper response?
options: Step back and offer a handshake instead
Lean in and gently touch cheeks, making a kissing sound
cultureQuiz.explanation: La bise involves gently touching cheeks (not actually kissing) and making a light kissing sound. It's a normal greeting among friends. Don't pull away or try to hug instead.

cultureQuiz.scenario: You're eating lunch at the university cafeteria and want to leave. What do you say?
cultureQuiz.explanation: Wishing others 'Bon appétit' when they're still eating and saying 'Au revoir' is polite. Simply leaving without a word is considered rude in French culture.

cultureQuiz.scenario: You just got great service at a restaurant. How much tip should you leave?
options: Service is included, but rounding up or leaving 1-2€ is a nice gesture
You must tip at least 10% or it's rude
cultureQuiz.explanation: In France, service charge (service compris) is included in the bill by law. Tipping is not required but leaving a few euros for excellent service is appreciated.

cultureQuiz.scenario: You want to buy groceries on Sunday afternoon. Where do you go?
options: A small épicerie (corner shop) or a supermarket open on Sunday mornings
The shopping mall, everything is open on Sundays
Order delivery since nothing is open
cultureQuiz.explanation: Most shops close on Sundays. Some supermarkets open Sunday mornings until 12-1 PM. Small épiceries (corner shops) in neighborhoods often stay open later. Plan your shopping accordingly!

cultureQuiz.scenario: A French person asks 'Ça va?' (How are you?). What's the expected response?
options: Give a detailed honest answer about your day
Ça va, merci! Et vous? (Fine, thanks! And you?)
I'm doing fantastic, let me tell you about my week!
cultureQuiz.explanation: 'Ça va?' is usually a polite formality, not an invitation for a long response. A quick 'Ça va, merci!' and returning the question is the standard polite exchange.

commonMistakes.correction: Use 'vous' with strangers, professors, and elders. 'Tu' is only for friends, family, and peers who invite you to use it.
Always say 'Bonjour' when meeting someone, entering a shop, or starting any interaction. It's the #1 rule of French politeness.
Always try French first, even if just 'Bonjour, parlez-vous anglais?' Many French people appreciate the effort and will switch to English if needed.
Keep your voice down on public transport, in restaurants, and on the street. The French tend to speak more quietly in public spaces.
Service is included in French restaurant bills. A small tip (1-2€) is appreciated but not expected. 20% would be excessive.

commonMistakes.mistake: Expecting everything to be open on Sundays
commonMistakes.correction: Most shops are closed on Sundays. Some supermarkets open Sunday mornings. Plan your shopping for weekdays or Saturday.
Always say goodbye when leaving a shop, office, or any interaction. 'Au revoir' or 'Bonne journée' is expected.

commonMistakes.mistake: Arriving on time for a dinner party
commonMistakes.correction: For social dinners, arriving 10-15 minutes late is the norm. Being exactly on time or early might inconvenience your host.
